title: Model Routing Is Simple. Until It Isn’t.
gist: IBM Research found that naive LLM-routing-by-price or by-difficulty fails in production agentic systems because caching economics, hidden task complexity, and serving infrastructure dominate real cost/latency outcomes; their optimization-based router beat both a difficulty-based router and running Opus alone on the AppWorld benchmark.

themes.theme: Hidden cost economics of model routing

insights.claim: In IBM Research's testing across 417 tasks on the AppWorld Test Challenge with a CodeAct agent, Claude Sonnet 4.6 cost $79 total ($0.19/task) versus GPT-4.1's $155 total ($0.37/task) — nearly double — despite GPT-4.1 having lower listed per-token pricing and Sonnet taking roughly three times as many reasoning steps per task.
insights.speaker: Yara Rizk
insights.so_what: Sticker-price token costs are a misleading input for model-routing or vendor-selection decisions in agentic workloads; investors evaluating AI infra spend or model-vendor competitiveness should weight cache-pricing structure, not headline token rates.
insights.quote: We expected GPT-4.1 to be cheaper than Claude Sonnet 4.6. It wasn’t.
insights.locator: Cost Is More Than Model Pricing

insights.claim: The cost inversion between Sonnet and GPT-4.1 was driven by caching: agent workloads reuse large chunks of context across steps, and Sonnet's lower cache-read pricing let it capture disproportionate savings from high cache-hit rates, enough to overcome both its higher base price and its longer (3x) reasoning trajectories.
insights.speaker: Yara Rizk
insights.so_what: Cache-pricing policy is becoming a real competitive lever between model vendors for agentic-workload market share — a factor investors should track when comparing Anthropic's vs OpenAI's effective enterprise pricing power, not just list prices.
insights.quote: A router that only looks at pricing sheets is optimizing against the wrong numbers.
insights.locator: Cost Is More Than Model Pricing

insights.claim: Task difficulty is often invisible at routing time in agentic systems: an apparently simple request like 'summarize this contract' can trigger retrieval, compliance checks, tool use, and multiple refinement rounds, while a technical-looking prompt may be handled efficiently by a smaller specialized model — so difficulty is frequently unknowable until execution is already underway.
insights.speaker: Yara Rizk
insights.so_what: Enterprises building AI cost-optimization pipelines should not rely on upfront difficulty classifiers alone; routing needs to be adaptive/mid-execution, which has implications for latency-cost tradeoffs in production agent deployments.
insights.quote: You often don’t know how hard a task actually is until execution is underway.
insights.locator: Complexity Is More Than Task Difficulty

insights.claim: Production model routers must simultaneously balance cost, quality, latency, model specialization, and reliability, plus enterprise governance constraints such as compliance requirements, data residency rules, privacy constraints, and approved-model allowlists — meaning the 'best' model for a task may be disallowed for non-technical reasons.
insights.speaker: Yara Rizk
insights.so_what: Enterprise AI routing is a governance-constrained optimization problem, not a pure ML classification task — vendors selling 'smart routing' products need compliance/data-residency features to be enterprise-credible, a differentiator investors should look for.
insights.quote: Routers aren’t solving one problem. They’re constantly juggling cost, quality, latency, compliance, and reliability all at once.
insights.locator: Complexity Is More Than Task Difficulty

themes.theme: Latency is a systems property, not a model property

insights.claim: End-to-end latency in production agent systems is dominated less by raw model size/speed than by infrastructure factors — which hardware a model runs on, whether the inference cache is warm, and how busy the serving endpoint is — so a theoretically faster model can still produce a slower real-world experience.
insights.speaker: Yara Rizk
insights.so_what: Benchmarked model-speed comparisons (common in vendor marketing) are poor proxies for real deployed latency; buyers should demand production serving-condition benchmarks, not lab throughput numbers.
insights.quote: A router that ignores the serving system is optimizing against the wrong reality.
insights.locator: Latency Is More Than Model Speed

insights.claim: Routing granularity is a direct latency-flexibility tradeoff: routing once per task adds minimal overhead, but routing at every step (which allows adapting model choice mid-execution) adds a latency and operational-complexity cost at every additional decision point.
insights.speaker: Yara Rizk
insights.so_what: A concrete design parameter for anyone building or evaluating agent-routing infrastructure: per-step routing sophistication must be weighed against its own latency tax.
insights.locator: Latency Is More Than Model Speed

insights.claim: IBM Research reframed model routing from a classification problem ('which model is best for this task?') to a systems optimization problem across cost, quality, and latency simultaneously, and built a lightweight router (~6ms and ~2kB memory overhead per task) that traces a cost-accuracy frontier of configurable operating points.
insights.speaker: Yara Rizk
insights.so_what: A reusable architectural pattern for AI infra vendors: routing-as-optimization rather than routing-as-classification is likely to become the competitive standard, relevant for evaluating LLM-gateway/router startups.
insights.quote: we stopped treating routing as a classification problem and started treating it as an optimization problem
insights.locator: So How Did We Handle This?

insights.claim: On the AppWorld Test Challenge, IBM's latency-optimized router configuration achieved 84% accuracy for $93 and 83 seconds — a 21% cost reduction and 9% latency reduction versus running Claude Opus alone, at the cost of only a 4-percentage-point accuracy drop; a second configuration pushed cost even lower.
insights.speaker: Yara Rizk
insights.so_what: Quantifies achievable cost savings (~20%+) from intelligent routing versus a single frontier model with minimal accuracy loss — a concrete benchmark investors can use to size the addressable savings opportunity for AI-routing tooling.
insights.locator: So How Did We Handle This?

insights.claim: A standard difficulty-based router lands in a similar accuracy range as IBM's optimization-based router but at higher cost, because it fails to explore the full cost-latency-accuracy tradeoff space that an optimization-based approach can access.
insights.speaker: Yara Rizk
insights.so_what: Directly undercuts the common industry assumption that difficulty-estimation routers are sufficient — a contrarian data point for anyone benchmarking or building LLM router products.
insights.quote: Notice that a standard difficulty-based router (the teal diamond) lands in a similar accuracy range but at higher cost — it doesn't explore the full tradeoff space the way an optimization-based approach can.
insights.locator: So How Did We Handle This?
